answer:
1. Definition & Core Concept

MongoDB Aggregation deep dive covers the aggregation pipeline framework: stages ($match, $group, $sort, $lookup, $unwind, $project, $facet), pipeline optimization rules, memory limits (100MB per stage), and the trade-offs versus MapReduce or client-side aggregation. At interviewer grade, you must explain how the aggregation pipeline optimizer reorders stages, when $lookup becomes a bottleneck (no index on foreign collection), and failure modes like exceeding the 100MB stage limit or running unindexed $match stages.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the CRM Sales Dashboard (MongoDB/Express), the pipeline analytics page required: leads grouped by stage, total deal value per assignee, conversion rates over time. Manoj built a $facet aggregation that ran three sub-pipelines in parallel: (1) $group by stage with $sum of dealValue, (2) $group by assigneeId with count and avg deal size, (3) $group by $dateToString for monthly trends. Trade-off: $facet scans the collection once but all sub-pipelines share the 100MB memory limit. Failure mode: the $lookup from Leads to Users (to get assignee names) was slow because the Users collection had no index on _id used in the foreignField — actually, _id is always indexed, but a $lookup on a non-_id field (like email) would be catastrophic. Rollback plan: if aggregation exceeds memory, add allowDiskUse: true (trades speed for correctness).

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// CRM Dashboard — Faceted aggregation pipeline
app.get('/api/dashboard/analytics', async (req) => {
  const { startDate, endDate } = req.query;

  const results = await Lead.aggregate([
    // Stage 1: Filter by date (uses index on createdAt)
    { $match: {
      createdAt: { $gte: new Date(startDate), $lte: new Date(endDate) },
    }},

    // Stage 2: Three parallel aggregations
    { $facet: {
      byStage: [
        { $group: {
          _id: '$stage',
          count: { $sum: 1 },
          totalValue: { $sum: '$dealValue' },
        }},
        { $sort: { totalValue: -1 } },
      ],
      byAssignee: [
        { $group: {
          _id: '$assigneeId',
          count: { $sum: 1 },
          avgDeal: { $avg: '$dealValue' },
        }},
        { $sort: { avgDeal: -1 } },
        { $limit: 10 }, // Top 10 performers
      ],
      byMonth: [
        { $group: {
          _id: { $dateToString: { format: '%Y-%m', date: '$createdAt' } },
          count: { $sum: 1 },
          wonValue: {
            $sum: { $cond: [{ $eq: ['$stage', 'won'] }, '$dealValue', 0] },
          },
        }},
        { $sort: { '_id': 1 } },
      ],
    }},
  ]).allowDiskUse(true); // Safety net for large datasets

  return results[0];
});

// Explain to check index usage
const explained = await Lead.aggregate([
  { $match: { createdAt: { $gte: startDate } } },
  { $group: { _id: '$stage', count: { $sum: 1 } } },
]).explain('executionStats');
console.log(explained.stages[0].$cursor.executionStats);

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The CRM dashboard's analytics endpoint took 3 seconds with 10k leads. Manoj ran .explain('executionStats') and found the $match stage was doing a COLLSCAN (collection scan) because the compound index was on {assigneeId: 1, updatedAt: -1} but the query filtered on createdAt. Adding an index on {createdAt: 1} dropped the first stage from 2.5s to 50ms. The remaining 500ms was in the $facet stage — acceptable for 10k documents. For 1M+ leads, Manoj planned to pre-compute the dashboard stats into a separate dashboard_stats collection via a change stream listener, making the API query a simple findOne (< 5ms).

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
$match after $groupFull collection scan before filteringAlways put $match first; optimizer moves it but verify with explain
$lookup on unindexed fieldNested loop join → O(n×m)Index the foreignField in the joined collection
Exceeding 100MB stage limitAggregation fails with memory errorallowDiskUse(true) or pre-filter with $match to reduce working set
$unwind on large arraysDocument explosion (1 doc → 1000 docs)Use $slice or $filter before $unwind to limit array size
No explain() during developmentSilent collection scans in productionRun explain() on every new pipeline; assert IXSCAN for $match

7. Validation Strategy

  • Explain test: Every aggregation pipeline must show IXSCAN for $match stage in explain output.
  • Memory test: Run pipeline against 100k documents without allowDiskUse; verify it completes within 100MB.
  • Performance budget: Dashboard analytics < 500ms for 50k leads, < 2s for 500k leads.
  • Pre-computation test: Compare aggregation result with pre-computed collection; assert identical values.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling MongoDB Aggregation for 1 M+ users requires moving beyond real-time aggregation pipelines to pre-computed views, sharded aggregation, and the $merge stage for incremental materialization. At 1 M+ documents, a $facet pipeline can take 10+ seconds — unacceptable for an interactive dashboard. The architecture must compute analytics asynchronously and serve pre-computed results to users.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

Scaling the CRM Sales Dashboard to 1 M+ leads, Manoj would architect: (1) Change stream listener on the Leads collection that triggers re-computation whenever a lead is created/updated. (2) $merge stage: the aggregation pipeline writes results to a dashboard_stats collection, replacing previous stats. (3) API serves pre-computed data: GET /api/dashboard reads from dashboard_stats (single document, < 5ms). (4) Sharding: if the Leads collection exceeds 100GB, shard by assigneeId (hashed) to distribute aggregation across shards. Each shard runs a partial aggregation, and mongos merges results.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Pre-computed dashboard stats with change stream + $merge
import mongoose from 'mongoose';

// Materialized view schema
const DashboardStats = mongoose.model('DashboardStats', new mongoose.Schema({
  _id: String, // 'global' or departmentId
  byStage: [{ stage: String, count: Number, totalValue: Number }],
  byAssignee: [{ assigneeId: String, count: Number, avgDeal: Number }],
  byMonth: [{ month: String, count: Number, wonValue: Number }],
  updatedAt: Date,
}));

// Re-compute stats (triggered by change stream)
async function recomputeStats() {
  await Lead.aggregate([
    { $facet: {
      byStage: [
        { $group: { _id: '$stage', count: { $sum: 1 }, totalValue: { $sum: '$dealValue' } } },
      ],
      byAssignee: [
        { $group: { _id: '$assigneeId', count: { $sum: 1 }, avgDeal: { $avg: '$dealValue' } } },
        { $sort: { avgDeal: -1 } },
        { $limit: 20 },
      ],
      byMonth: [
        { $group: {
          _id: { $dateToString: { format: '%Y-%m', date: '$createdAt' } },
          count: { $sum: 1 },
          wonValue: { $sum: { $cond: [{ $eq: ['$stage', 'won'] }, '$dealValue', 0] } },
        }},
        { $sort: { _id: 1 } },
      ],
    }},
    { $addFields: { _id: 'global', updatedAt: new Date() } },
    { $merge: { into: 'dashboardstats', whenMatched: 'replace', whenNotMatched: 'insert' } },
  ]).allowDiskUse(true);
}

// Change stream triggers re-computation (debounced)
const changeStream = Lead.watch();
let debounceTimer: NodeJS.Timeout;
changeStream.on('change', () => {
  clearTimeout(debounceTimer);
  debounceTimer = setTimeout(recomputeStats, 5000); // Max every 5s
});

// API: instant read from pre-computed collection
app.get('/api/dashboard', async (req) => {
  const stats = await DashboardStats.findById('global').lean();
  return { ...stats, staleness: Date.now() - stats.updatedAt.getTime() };
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

With 50k leads, the CRM dashboard's real-time aggregation took 800ms — noticeable lag on every page load. Manoj implemented the pre-computation pattern: a change stream listener debounced to re-run the aggregation at most every 5 seconds. The dashboard API now read from the dashboardstats collection (single findOne, 3ms). The trade-off: stats could be up to 5 seconds stale, which was perfectly acceptable for sales reporting. The UI showed "Updated 3s ago" to set expectations. For the rare case where a manager needed real-time numbers (e.g., closing a deal), a "Refresh Now" button triggered the aggregation synchronously.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Real-time aggregation on 1M+ docs10+ second query for every dashboard loadPre-compute via $merge + change stream
Change stream not debounced100 writes/s → 100 aggregations/sDebounce to max 1 re-computation per 5s
$merge without allowDiskUseMemory error on large collectionsAlways enable allowDiskUse for materialization
Scatter-gather on unsharded collectionSingle mongod handles all aggregation workShard by hashed key for parallel processing
Stale stats without indicatorUsers make decisions on outdated dataShow staleness indicator + manual refresh button

7. Validation Strategy

  • Latency test: Dashboard API reads pre-computed stats; assert < 10ms response time.
  • Staleness test: Create a lead, wait 5s, verify stats collection updated (debounce window).
  • Scale test: Run materialization on 1M leads; assert completes within 30s with allowDiskUse.
  • Accuracy test: Compare pre-computed stats with live aggregation; assert identical results.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling Mongoose Modeling for 1 M+ users requires schema design decisions that impact query performance, memory usage, and write throughput: embedding vs. referencing, bucket pattern for time-series data, polymorphic patterns for varying document shapes, and strategic denormalization for read-heavy paths. At scale, a deeply nested embedded document that was convenient at 1k documents becomes a write amplifier at 1M — every update to a nested field rewrites the entire document.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In ContactsHub (React/Fastify/MongoDB), contacts had nested arrays for phone numbers, emails, and tags. At small scale (100 contacts), embedding worked fine. For 1 M+ contacts, Manoj would restructure: (1) Embed small arrays (phone numbers — max 5) because they're read with every contact query. (2) Reference large arrays (activity log — unbounded) into a separate ContactActivity collection to prevent document growth beyond 16MB. (3) Bucket pattern for the activity log: group activities by day into bucket documents, reducing the document count from 1M individual activities to ~3k daily buckets. In the CRM Dashboard, leads were embedded with their stage history (bounded to ~10 entries) but referenced their notes (unbounded) in a separate collection.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// ContactsHub — Mongoose schema at scale
import mongoose from 'mongoose';

// Embedded: bounded arrays read with every contact
const ContactSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: { type: String, required: true, index: true },
  phones: [{ type: String, maxlength: 15 }], // Max 5, always shown
  emails: [{ type: String, maxlength: 100 }], // Max 3
  tags: [{ type: String }],                   // Max 20
  avatar: String,                              // Cloudinary URL
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now, index: true },
});

// Referenced: unbounded activity log
const ActivityBucketSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  contactId: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'Contact', index: true },
  date: { type: String, index: true }, // '2025-03-09'
  activities: [{
    type: { type: String, enum: ['call', 'email', 'meeting', 'note'] },
    detail: String,
    timestamp: Date,
  }],
  count: { type: Number, default: 0 },
});

// Compound index for efficient bucket queries
ActivityBucketSchema.index({ contactId: 1, date: -1 });

const Contact = mongoose.model('Contact', ContactSchema);
const ActivityBucket = mongoose.model('ActivityBucket', ActivityBucketSchema);

// Add activity using bucket pattern (upsert)
async function addActivity(contactId, activity) {
  const date = new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0];
  await ActivityBucket.updateOne(
    { contactId, date },
    {
      $push: { activities: { ...activity, timestamp: new Date() } },
      $inc: { count: 1 },
    },
    { upsert: true } // Create bucket if doesn't exist for today
  );
}

// Read recent activities (last 7 days)
async function getRecentActivities(contactId) {
  const weekAgo = new Date(Date.now() - 7 * 86400000).toISOString().split('T')[0];
  return ActivityBucket.find({
    contactId,
    date: { $gte: weekAgo },
  }).sort({ date: -1 }).lean();
}

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

In ContactsHub, Manoj initially embedded the entire activity log as an array inside the Contact document. After a contact had 500+ activities, the document grew to 200KB — every time Manoj fetched the contact list (which only needed name, phone, avatar), it loaded the entire activity array too. MongoDB's projection ({ activities: 0 }) excluded the field but the document was still loaded from disk in full. The fix: moving activities to a referenced ActivityBucket collection kept the Contact document at ~2KB. The contact list query went from 50ms (loading 100 × 200KB documents) to 8ms (loading 100 × 2KB documents). Activities were loaded on-demand when the user clicked into a specific contact.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Embedding unbounded arraysDocument exceeds 16MB limit; slow readsReference into separate collection
Projecting out large fieldsMongoDB still reads full doc from diskMove large fields to separate collection
One document per activity1M tiny documents → index overheadBucket pattern groups by time period
populate() on list queriesN+1 queries for N contactsUse $lookup in aggregation or denormalize needed fields
No index on referenced field$lookup does collection scanIndex contactId in referenced collection

7. Validation Strategy

  • Document size test: Assert average Contact document size < 5KB (no embedded unbounded arrays).
  • List performance: Fetch 100 contacts; assert < 20ms (small documents).
  • Bucket test: Add 1000 activities to one contact; verify ~1 bucket per day (not 1000 individual docs).
  • 16MB limit test: Simulate 10k activities per contact; verify no BSONObjectTooLarge error.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Mongoose Modeling performance bottlenecks occur at the schema level: middleware (pre/post hooks) executing on every save, virtual fields computed on every toJSON, populate() causing N+1 queries, lean() not used for read-only queries (Mongoose hydration overhead), and missing indexes on commonly queried fields. Instrumenting these requires measuring query times, document sizes, and middleware execution overhead.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, the Lead model had 5 pre-save hooks (validation, slug generation, audit timestamp, stage history push, assignee notification). Each save took 15ms in hooks alone — at 100 lead updates/second, that's 1.5 seconds of CPU per second just on hooks. Manoj profiled by wrapping each hook with performance.now() and found the assignee notification hook (which queried the User collection) was the bottleneck (8ms). Fix: moved notification to a post-save hook that fired asynchronously, and used .lean() on all read-only queries (list views, reports) to skip Mongoose hydration (saving 2ms per document × 100 documents = 200ms per page load).

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Instrumented Mongoose model for CRM Leads
import mongoose from 'mongoose';

const LeadSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: String,
  email: { type: String, index: true },
  stage: { type: String, index: true },
  assigneeId: { type: mongoose.Schema.Types.ObjectId, ref: 'User', index: true },
  dealValue: Number,
  assigneeName: String, // Denormalized for list views
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now },
});

// Compound index for common query patterns
LeadSchema.index({ assigneeId: 1, stage: 1, createdAt: -1 });

// Move slow notification to async post-save
LeadSchema.post('save', function (doc) {
  // Fire-and-forget — doesn't block save response
  notifyAssignee(doc.assigneeId, doc).catch(console.error);
});

// Denormalize assignee name on assignment (avoid populate on list)
LeadSchema.pre('save', async function () {
  if (this.isModified('assigneeId')) {
    const user = await mongoose.model('User').findById(this.assigneeId, 'name').lean();
    this.assigneeName = user?.name || 'Unassigned';
  }
});

const Lead = mongoose.model('Lead', LeadSchema);

// Read-only list: lean() skips Mongoose hydration
app.get('/api/leads', async (req) => {
  return Lead.find({ assigneeId: req.user.id })
    .select('name email stage dealValue assigneeName createdAt')
    .sort({ createdAt: -1 })
    .limit(50)
    .lean() // Returns plain JS objects — 2x faster than Mongoose documents
    .exec();
});

// Enable query profiling
mongoose.set('debug', (collectionName, method, query, doc, options) => {
  console.log(`${collectionName}.${method}`, JSON.stringify(query));
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The CRM lead list page took 400ms for 100 leads. Manoj profiled and found three bottlenecks: (1) populate('assigneeId') caused 100 individual User queries (N+1). Fix: denormalized assigneeName into the Lead document on assignment, eliminating all User queries. (2) Mongoose hydration added 2ms per document (200ms for 100). Fix: added .lean() since the list view only displayed data (no document methods needed). (3) No compound index on {assigneeId, stage, createdAt} — the query did a COLLSCAN. Fix: added the compound index. Combined savings: 400ms → 35ms (11x improvement).

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
populate() on list queriesN+1 queries for N documentsDenormalize or use $lookup in aggregation
No lean() for read-only queries2ms hydration overhead per documentUse .lean() for all read-only queries
Synchronous pre-save hooks with I/OBlocks save response with DB queriesMove to async post-save or event-driven
Missing compound indexesCOLLSCAN on filtered + sorted queriesCreate compound index matching query filter + sort
Virtuals computed on every toJSONCPU overhead for computed fieldsStore computed values on write (materialized)

7. Validation Strategy

  • Query count test: List endpoint must execute ≤ 1 MongoDB query (no populate N+1).
  • Lean test: Assert list endpoint returns plain objects (no Mongoose document methods).
  • Hook profiling: Measure total hook execution time; assert < 5ms per save.
  • Index test: Run explain() on list query; assert IXSCAN not COLLSCAN.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Data Partitioning at scale splits large datasets across multiple storage units — horizontal partitioning (sharding) distributes rows across nodes, vertical partitioning separates columns into different tables, and functional partitioning splits by use case (OLTP vs analytics). For 1 M+ users, a single PostgreSQL table with 100M rows becomes a bottleneck: B-tree indexes grow deep, VACUUM takes hours, and write contention increases. Partitioning keeps each partition manageable (< 10M rows) with independent indexes and maintenance.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

Scaling the HR Leave Management System to 1 M+ users with 100M+ leave records, Manoj would partition the Leave table by createdAt (range partitioning by month): Leave_2025_01, Leave_2025_02, etc. Queries filtering by date range (90% of dashboard queries) scan only relevant partitions. For the CRM Sales Dashboard (MongoDB), sharding by assigneeId (hashed) distributes leads across shards — queries for a specific assignee hit a single shard (targeted query), while global analytics do scatter-gather across all shards.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

-- PostgreSQL range partitioning for Leave table
CREATE TABLE "Leave" (
  id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  "userId" UUID NOT NULL,
  "startDate" DATE NOT NULL,
  "endDate" DATE NOT NULL,
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
  "createdAt" TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  PRIMARY KEY (id, "createdAt")
) PARTITION BY RANGE ("createdAt");

-- Monthly partitions
CREATE TABLE leave_2025_01 PARTITION OF "Leave"
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-01-01') TO ('2025-02-01');
CREATE TABLE leave_2025_02 PARTITION OF "Leave"
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-02-01') TO ('2025-03-01');
-- ... auto-created by pg_partman

-- Indexes are per-partition (smaller, faster)
CREATE INDEX idx_leave_2025_01_user ON leave_2025_01("userId");
CREATE INDEX idx_leave_2025_01_status ON leave_2025_01(status)
  WHERE status = 'pending';

-- Query: partition pruning happens automatically
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM "Leave"
WHERE "createdAt" >= '2025-02-01'
  AND "createdAt" < '2025-03-01'
  AND "userId" = 'abc';
-- Output: Scan only leave_2025_02 partition

-- MongoDB sharding for CRM leads
// sh.enableSharding('crmdb');
// sh.shardCollection('crmdb.leads', { assigneeId: 'hashed' });

// Targeted query (single shard)
const myLeads = await Lead.find({ assigneeId: userId }).lean();

// Scatter-gather (all shards)
const globalStats = await Lead.aggregate([
  { $group: { _id: '$stage', count: { $sum: 1 } } },
]);

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

In a stress test simulating 2 years of HR data (500k leave records), the Leave table's B-tree index on userId grew to 200MB, and VACUUM took 45 minutes (blocking autovacuum for other tables). After partitioning by month, each partition's index was ~8MB, and VACUUM completed in 30 seconds per partition. The key insight: partition pruning meant that the dashboard query (WHERE createdAt >= current_month) only scanned 1 partition (~20k rows) instead of the full 500k-row table. Query time dropped from 200ms to 12ms. Manoj automated partition creation with a monthly cron job that ran CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS for the next month's partition.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
No partition pruning in queryScans all partitions (worse than no partitioning)Always include partition key in WHERE clause
Forgetting to create future partitionsINSERT fails with "no partition for value"Automate with pg_partman or cron job
Too many partitions (daily)Planner overhead scanning 365 partition defsMonthly partitions (12/year) is usually sufficient
Cross-partition queries (no key in WHERE)Sequential scan across all partitionsRedesign queries to include partition key
MongoDB hot shard (monotonic key)One shard gets all writesUse hashed shard key for even distribution

7. Validation Strategy

  • Pruning test: Run EXPLAIN on date-filtered query; assert only relevant partition is scanned.
  • Partition creation: Verify next month's partition exists before month end; alert if missing.
  • VACUUM test: Measure VACUUM duration per partition; assert < 5 minutes each.
  • Shard balance: MongoDB sh.status() shows chunk distribution; assert < 20% skew between shards.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Data Partitioning performance bottlenecks appear when the partitioning strategy doesn't match query patterns: cross-partition queries that scan all partitions, hot partitions where one partition receives disproportionate writes, partition key not included in queries (no pruning), and the overhead of managing hundreds of partitions (planner slowdown). Instrumenting requires monitoring partition sizes, query plan partition pruning, and write distribution across partitions.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, after partitioning by createdAt monthly, Manoj found that the "all pending leaves" query (WHERE status = 'pending' without a date filter) scanned all 24 partitions — worse than before partitioning because the planner checked each partition's metadata. Fix: added a date range to the query (AND createdAt >= NOW() - INTERVAL '3 months') which pruned to 3 partitions. For queries that genuinely needed all partitions (annual reports), Manoj used the pre-computed dashboard_stats table instead of scanning the partitioned table. In the CRM (MongoDB), a hot shard appeared because 60% of leads were assigned to 3 top salespeople — Manoj re-sharded with a hashed compound key {assigneeId: 'hashed', _id: 1} to spread even these hot assignees across shards.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

-- Monitor partition usage in PostgreSQL
SELECT relname AS partition,
       n_live_tup AS rows,
       seq_scan AS seq_scans,
       idx_scan AS idx_scans,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(oid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE relname LIKE 'leave_%'
ORDER BY relname;

-- Verify partition pruning in query plan
EXPLAIN (COSTS OFF)
SELECT * FROM "Leave"
WHERE "createdAt" >= '2025-02-01'
  AND "createdAt" < '2025-03-01'
  AND status = 'pending';
-- Expected: Scan on leave_2025_02 ONLY
-- Bad: Append → Scan on leave_2025_01, Scan on leave_2025_02, ...

-- Fix cross-partition query: always include partition key
-- Before (scans ALL partitions):
SELECT * FROM "Leave" WHERE status = 'pending';

-- After (scans 3 partitions):
SELECT * FROM "Leave"
WHERE status = 'pending'
  AND "createdAt" >= NOW() - INTERVAL '3 months';

// Fastify: enforce date range on leave queries
app.get('/api/leaves', async (req) => {
  const maxLookback = 90; // days
  const since = req.query.since || new Date(Date.now() - maxLookback * 86400000);
  return prisma.leave.findMany({
    where: {
      status: req.query.status,
      createdAt: { gte: since }, // Ensures partition pruning
      userId: req.user.id,
    },
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

After partitioning, the HR dashboard's "Pending Approvals" button showed all pending leaves regardless of date. The query had no createdAt filter, so PostgreSQL scanned all 24 monthly partitions — the EXPLAIN showed an Append node with 24 child scans. Performance was actually worse than the unpartitioned table because of the overhead of opening 24 partition files. Manoj added a business rule: pending leaves older than 90 days were auto-expired by a cron job, so the query could safely add createdAt >= NOW() - INTERVAL '3 months'. This pruned to 3 partitions, and the query returned in 15ms instead of 300ms.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Query without partition keyAll partitions scanned (Append node)Enforce partition key in all queries; API-level default date range
Too many small partitionsPlanner overhead scanning 1000+ partition defsUse monthly (not daily) partitions; merge old partitions
Hot current-month partitionAll writes hit one partition's indexesExpected for time-series; ensure partition has adequate I/O
No automated partition creationINSERT fails when new month startspg_partman or cron job creates partitions 3 months ahead
VACUUM runs on all partitionsAuto-vacuum blocks on large partitionConfigure autovacuum per-partition with tuned thresholds

7. Validation Strategy

  • Pruning CI test: Run EXPLAIN on top 10 queries; assert no Append node scanning all partitions.
  • Size monitoring: Alert when any partition exceeds 10GB (might need sub-partitioning).
  • Write distribution: Check current-month partition's write rate; alert if > 80% of total (expected but monitored).
  • Auto-creation test: Verify partitions exist for next 3 months; alert if missing.
